steps.title: Identify the key tension in the passage
steps.content:
Jane says she goes about her day "tranquilly" — outwardly calm — but "ever and anon vague suggestions kept wandering across my brain" about quitting. The word "but" signals the contrast between surface composure and inner restlessness. Her thoughts arise "involuntarily," confirming she cannot control them.
  • Choice A — To convey a contrast between Jane's outward calmness and internal restlessness: Matches the "tranquilly" vs. involuntary wandering thoughts structure of the passage exactly. ✓
  • Choice B — To emphasize Jane's loyalty to the people she works for at Thornfield Hall: The text never mentions her employers or any feelings toward them — she is thinking about leaving. ✗
  • Choice C — To demonstrate that Jane finds her situation both challenging and deeply fulfilling: Nothing in the text suggests fulfillment; every detail points to discontent and thoughts of escape. ✗
  • Choice D — To describe Jane's determination to secure employment outside of Thornfield Hall: Jane says her thoughts "might germinate and bear fruit if they could" — a conditional, not a determination. She has not decided to leave. ✗

steps.title: Confirm the contrast
steps.content: "Tranquilly" (calm exterior) set against involuntary, wandering thoughts of quitting (restless interior) is the passage's entire purpose. That matches Choice A — To convey a contrast between Jane's outward calmness and internal restlessness.
